questions.question: What is BIO tagging?
options: Per-token labels: B-TYPE for entity start, I-TYPE for inside, O for outside
A binary entity vs non-entity scheme
questions.explanation: BIO turns span extraction into token classification with B/I/O prefixes.

questions.question: Why are rule-based gazetteers brittle in production NER?
options: They have zero coverage on new entities and cannot disambiguate (e.g. Apple fruit vs company)
questions.explanation: Gazetteers can match known strings but cannot disambiguate sense or generalize to unseen names.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of a CRF over an HMM for NER?
options: CRFs are discriminative and can mix arbitrary features (shape, capitalization, neighbors)
questions.explanation: CRFs are discriminative and let you condition on rich, overlapping features.

questions.question: In a BiLSTM-CRF architecture, what role does the CRF layer play?
options: Enforces valid BIO tag sequences by modeling tag-to-tag transitions on top of LSTM emissions
questions.explanation: The CRF on top of LSTM features models inter-label dependencies and rules out illegal sequences.

questions.question: Why must NER be evaluated with entity-level F1, not token-level F1?
options: Entity-level F1 is easier to compute
Predicted spans must match true spans exactly; token-level F1 overstates accuracy by counting partial matches
Entity-level F1 is required by HuggingFace
questions.explanation: Span exact-match is the meaningful metric; token-level F1 inflates scores via partial overlap.

questions.question: What does aggregation_strategy='simple' do in the HuggingFace NER pipeline?
options: Merges contiguous B-X and I-X tokens into a single span
Returns only the most confident entity
questions.explanation: It merges contiguous BIO tokens of the same type into span-level entities.

questions.question: Why does standard BIO fail on nested entities?
options: BIO is a flat per-token scheme and cannot express two overlapping spans of different types
questions.explanation: BIO assigns one label per token; nested spans need multi-pass or span-based models.

questions.question: When does classical NER (CRF or BiLSTM-CRF) still beat an LLM in 2026?
options: Whenever the input is in English
Under tight latency budgets, abundant labels, stable ontologies, or non-generative regulatory constraints
questions.explanation: Classical NER wins on latency, labeled-data regimes, fixed ontologies, and on-prem constraints.
